coredata.dc:title: Planar and dendrite-free zinc deposition enabled by exposed crystal plane optimization of zinc anode
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tian
Sun, Jinmeng
Hua, Yongbin
Krishna, Bolisetti Naga Vamsi
Xi, Qiao
Ai, Wei
Yu, Jae Su
coredata.dc:description: The low Coulombic efficiency and limited cycle life of zinc (Zn) metal anode resulting from the severe side reactions and dendrite growth are the major bottlenecks restricting the commercial applications of rechargeable aqueous Zn metal batteries (ZMBs). Considering that the crystal orientation of the electrode surface determines the growth direction of the newly deposited metal, however, limited by the crystal heterogeneity of commercial Zn foil, it can easily lead to inhomogeneous deposition morphology. Therefore, Zn electrode with more exposed (002) plane is considered as an effective strategy for planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition. In this review, we first provide the advantages of the preferred Zn (002) plane for achieving flat Zn deposition and elucidate the effect of electrode surface crystal orientation on Zn metal deposition behavior by correlating crystallography and deposition morphology. Then, we summarize the recent progress in the design and optimization strategies for directional deposition of Zn metal along the (002) orientation. Finally, the challenges, potential solutions, and perspectives for further exploration of planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition are proposed, which are expected to spur more insightful works toward advanced aqueous ZMBs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The low Coulombic efficiency and limited cycle life of zinc (Zn) metal anode resulting from the severe side reactions and dendrite growth are the major bottlenecks restricting the commercial applications of rechargeable aqueous Zn metal batteries (ZMBs). Considering that the crystal orientation of the electrode surface determines the growth direction of the newly deposited metal, however, limited by the crystal heterogeneity of commercial Zn foil, it can easily lead to inhomogeneous deposition morphology. Therefore, Zn electrode with more exposed (002) plane is considered as an effective strategy for planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition. In this review, we first provide the advantages of the preferred Zn (002) plane for achieving flat Zn deposition and elucidate the effect of electrode surface crystal orientation on Zn metal deposition behavior by correlating crystallography and deposition morphology. Then, we summarize the recent progress in the design and optimization strategies for directional deposition of Zn metal along the (002) orientation. Finally, the challenges, potential solutions, and perspectives for further exploration of planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition are proposed, which are expected to spur more insightful works toward advanced aqueous ZMBs.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of lithium (Li)-ion batteries (LIBs) has achieved great success during the past three decades, and their application in the field of portable electronic devices and electric vehicles has had a profound change in the lifestyles of humans [1–4]. However, the limited Li reservoir and potential safety issues hinder the further development of LIBs [5–8]. Aqueous rechargeable batteries have attracted increasing attention due to their intrinsic safety, low cost, and environmental friendliness [9–12]. Moreover, the ionic conductivity of aqueous electrolytes (∼0.1 S cm−1) is higher than that of organic electrolytes (∼1-10 mS cm−1), which enables excellent electrochemical reaction kinetics and high power density [13–15]. Among them, aqueous rechargeable zinc (Zn) metal batteries (ZMBs) have received much interest due to the merits of metallic Zn stemming from its high theoretical capacity (820 Ah Kg−1, 5854 Ah L−1), resource abundance, and low reduction potential (-0.76 V vs. the standard hydrogen electrode) [11,16-18].

Despite the numerous advantages, the utilization of Zn metal anode still faces some enormous challenges. The main issues related to the Zn metal anode are as follows (Fig. 1 ). (1) The standard electrode potential of Zn/Zn2+ is lower than that of H2/H+, and the thermodynamic instability of Zn in water-based electrolytes generates the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and chemical corrosion that continuously consume electrolyte and active Zn metal, resulting in a decrease in the Coulombic efficiency (CE) and poor cycle life [11,18-22]. Moreover, H2 evolution increases the pressure in the sealed battery system, which may cause electrolyte leakage and even explosion [23–25]. (2) In metallic Zn as a hostless electrode, the Zn ions execute dynamically plating/stripping at the electrode-electrolyte interface during the cycling and accompany the redistribution of the Zn ions [26]. However, the inhomogeneous distribution of Zn-ion flux and electrical field at the electrode-electrolyte interface induces the Zn ions to form rough and non-uniform deposition, promoting the growth of dendrites [21,27]. Zn has a high Young's modulus (EZn ≈ 108 GPa), and Zn dendrites more easily proliferate, which means that Zn dendrites will grow fast once formed [27–29], thereby resulting in irreversible failure, or even separator puncture that causes the battery short circuit [30,31]. (3) Due to the strong electrostatic interactions of divalent Zn ions, the rigid and tight solvation sheath makes it difficult for Zn ions to desolve when they pass through the electrode-electrolyte interface, causing sluggish Zn diffusion kinetics and various side reactions [18,32,33]. Feedback to high overpotential, low CE, and other phenomena are detrimental to long-term battery stability [33–36]. Intensive works have been done to address these Zn-related issues in recent years (Fig. 1b), such as selecting and designing the architecture of current collectors [37–42], introducing artificial interfacial layers [25,33,35,43-47], adjusting the exposed crystal plane of the Zn metal anode, [48–50] electrolyte engineering, [17, 51-55] and separator modification [56–58]. There is no doubt that the implementation of those strategies has had positive effects to withstand long-term Zn plating/stripping.

Considering that the crystal orientation of the electrode induces the growth direction of the newly deposited metal, this means that the crystallographic heterogeneity of the metal electrode translates into the heterogeneity of the deposited metal morphology [28,46,48,59,60]. Therefore, the surface crystal homogeneity of the metal electrode is important to guide long-term planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition. Meanwhile, the efficient and sustained regulation of the deposited metal is also a non-negligible factor for stable Zn plating/stripping [61]. Specifically, Zn is a typical hexagonal close-packed (hcp) structure (Fig. 2 a). In the (002), (100), and (101) crystal planes with high relative texture coefficients (RTCs), the preferred crystal plane determines the final morphology of the newly deposited Zn metal [48,62]. In detail, when the preferred crystal planes of the Zn substrate consist of (100) and (101), the deposited Zn flakes are more inclined to grow at a large angle (about 70-90o with the substrate), which will induce the growth of Zn dendrites[63,64]. However, when the preferred crystal plane of the Zn substrate consists of (002), the deposited Zn flakes are more inclined to grow at a small angle (about 0-30o with the substrate), which enables the Zn metal with a uniform deposition (Fig. 2b) [27,63]. Therefore, the use of advanced strategies to achieve Zn metal planarization deposition is considered a promising approach to improve the electrochemical performance of Zn anode.

In this review, we focus on the effect of crystal orientation on the Zn metal deposition behavior and the design and optimization strategies of the Zn (002) plane by correlating the crystallography and deposition morphology. The importance of more exposed (002) plane for flat Zn metal deposition and improved electrochemical performance of Zn electrodes are emphasized. The research progress of realizing the preferred (002) plane of Zn anode in aqueous ZMBs is summarized, including the construction of an artificial interfacial layer, the optimization of electrolyte and separator, and the design of textured Zn electrode (Fig. 2c). Meanwhile, the functions of these specific strategies to achieve (002) plane-oriented deposition during Zn metal deposition are analyzed in detail. Finally, we look forward to the prospects and challenges of planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition on the Zn (002) basal plane, aiming to provide some clues and rational strategies for the future development of highly reversible ZMBs.

Controlled Zn deposition refers to the process of epitaxial growth of Zn metal on substrates with lattice-matched interfaces for achieving homogeneous and planar Zn deposition [28,65]. Due to the hostless nature of Zn metal anode, the surface atomic structure could affect the planting/stripping characteristics [49]. Therefore, the crystallographic orientation of the substrate will affect the nucleation sites of the newly deposited metal and the subsequent growth direction. From the perspective atomic arrangement of Zn structure, the surface atoms on the Zn (100) plane are arranged in a non-smooth with wave-like manner, whereas the atoms on the Zn (002) plane show a highly ordered and relatively smooth surface [49,54]. Therefore, the Zn (002) crystal plane has a smooth equipotential surface and a dense architecture, which supplies a favorable location for the flat Zn metal deposition.[30]

Early research experience has shown that the Zn (002) surface exhibited advantages over the Zn (100) and (101) planes in terms of corrosion resistance and dendrite suppression [64,66]. With the help of theoretical calculations, these highlights of the Zn (002) crystal plane can be reasonably explained from the energy point of view. It has been reported that the surface stability of inorganic crystals was dominated by surface energy [67]. In addition, the surface energy of the crystal had a close relationship with the preferential exposure of the crystal planes [68]. Therefore, Cao et al. calculated the surface energies of different crystal planes based on the atomic arrangement of the (002) and (101) planes of Zn, and the results showed that the (002) plane had lower surface energy [69]. Qiu et al. calculated the surface energies of Zn (002), (100), and (101) planes, which also displayed the lowest surface energy of the Zn (002) plane [70]. Furthermore, Zhou et al. performed more detailed theoretical simulations of the Zn adsorption and dissolution at different sites on the Zn (100) and (002) crystal planes [49]. As shown in the density functional theory (DFT) caculation of Fig. 3 a, Zn ion preferred to form individual site on the top of the Zn (100) plane (corresponding to site 2 of the Zn (100) plane), which evolved into vertically aligned Zn flakes of the substrate during subsequent deposition. Compared with the Zn (002) plane, the adsorption energy of the Zn ion at the edge site (corresponding to site 1 of the Zn (002) plane) was lower than that at the top site, indicating that the Zn deposited on the Zn (002) plane tended to be parallel to the substrate, thereby achieving dendrite-free Zn deposition. Meanwhile, considering the effect of HER in ZMBs on the CE of Zn metal anode, the free energy of H adsorption on Zn anode was investigated. The results revealed that the free energy of H adsorption on the Zn (002) plane was higher than that on the Zn (100) plane, indicating that the Zn (002) plane could effectively inhibit HER (Fig. 3b). Fig. 3c shows the energy of the Zn dissolution from different crystal planes. The higher stripping energy of the Zn from the (002) plane than that of the Zn from the (100) plane indicated that the Zn atoms arranged on the (002) plane had a strong interaction force, which implied that the Zn (002) surface significantly resisted the side reactions.[71]

During the metal deposition, the migration of metal ions to the electrode surface is driven by the electric field [72]. In addition, the electric field distribution and the ion concentration gradient on the electrode surface determine the subsequent metal growth [73–75]. According to the analysis of the binding energy of Zn with different crystal planes, it can be found that when Zn ions nucleate on non-(002) crystal planes, the exposed crystal planes induce the formation of Zn dendrites, driven by surface energy minimization [27]. Meanwhile, the electric field distribution and ion concentration gradient on the electrode surface will also change, which accelerates the further growth of subsequent dendrites. As shown in Fig. 3d and e, Yang et al. simulated the changes of the electric field distribution and ion concentration gradient of the Zn electrode surface protrusions [76]. It could be seen that the electric field distribution at the protruding parts of the electrode surface was uneven and accompanied by a trend of increasing intensity (Fig. 3d). In addition, the Zn ion concentration field simulation showed that with the increase of Zn deposition time, the surface protrusions increased rapidly, so more Zn ions gathered around the protrusions, forming an obvious concentration gradient (Fig. 3e). Moreover, Hong et al. further simulated the evolution of Zn dendrites during Zn deposition with the overpotential of 200 mV [77]. In the early stage of nucleation, the electrode morphology evolution mainly involved small fluctuations on the electrode surface, and then the small nucleation sites gradually protruded into filaments and evolved into dendritic structures in the subsequent deposition process (Fig. 3f). Obviously, with the growth of dendrites, the ion concentration gradient and the inhomogeneity of the electric field distribution further deteriorate, and the hydrogen evolution problem of Zn metal in the electrolyte is also exacerbated (Fig. 3g), leading to the formation of dendrites and the decrease in the CE. More seriously, the high Young's modulus will puncture the separator and make the battery to cause a short circuit.

Therefore, the preferred (002) crystal plane on the Zn substrate can realize the flat deposition of Zn atoms in the early stage of nucleation, thereby eliminating the dendrite nucleation. Meanwhile, it can delay the occurrence of side reactions on the electrode surface and improve the CE of the Zn metal anode and the cycling life of the ZMBs. Furthermore, the planar Zn deposition reduces the risk of dendrite growth caused by uneven field strength distribution and ion concentration gradient of the electrode-electrolyte surface.

According to the metal deposition model, the distribution of metal ions at the electrode-electrolyte interface affects the final morphology of the deposited metal [73,78]. However, the passivation layer on the Zn metal surface in an aqueous electrolyte is generally uneven and instable [45]. Furthermore, the Zn salt anions are electrochemically stable in the solution, which cannot form a Zn-ion conducting solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) [79]. The result is a heterogeneous distribution of Zn-ion flux on the electrode surface, inducing the growth of dendrites during the continuous plating/stripping[80–82]. Based on the successful experience of Li/Na metal batteries, it is a promising strategy to construct an effective artificial interfacial layer on Zn anode to achieve uniform Zn deposition [83–87]. Remarkably, an ideal artificial interface layer should have the following features. (1) The structure and performance can be easily adjusted and tailored, but almost unaffected during electrochemical processes [88]. (2) It provides a unique ion channel for fast ion diffusion kinetics [21,31]. (3) This architecture can prevent direct contact between Zn metal and the electrolyte and thus avoid excessive corrosion of the electrodes and electrolyte consumption [84,85]. (4) Excellent mechanical strength inhibits the growth of dendrites [31,47]. Generally, there are two major strategies to construct artificial interfacial layers which involve building on the Zn metal surface through direct surface coating and chemical/electrochemical reactions.

Considering that the electrodeposition of Zn metal on the electrode belongs to epitaxial growth, the similar atomic arrangement of the substrate and the Zn (002) crystal plane is beneficial to realizing the long-term planar Zn deposition. Therefore, an interfacial layer with a low lattice mismatch to the Zn (002) plane becomes a preferred strategy. Zheng et al. calculated that the lattice mismatch between graphene (002) and Zn (002) crystal planes is δ ≈ 7%, and this low lattice mismatch efficiently guided the newly deposited metal to be deposited parallel to the substrate direction [28]. At a current density of 40 mA cm−2, the CE of the graphene-coated electrode remained at 99.9% after 10,000 cycles, indicating that the reversibility of the electrode was considerably improved. Meanwhile, the two-dimensional (2D) grazing incident X-ray diffraction (GIXRD) patterns of Zn electrodeposits revealed the homo-epitaxial Zn deposition guided by the graphene interfacial layer. In pursuit of low interfacial resistance and fast ion migration kinetics, the establishment of nanoscale artificial interfacial layers is considered a preferred idea [43,89]. However, the obtained coating layer by a doctor blade method is generally microscale and it is difficult to achieve a nanoscale coating layer. Therefore, Zhou et al. reported a one-step Langmuir-Blodgett method to obtain an ultrathin nitrogen (N)-doped graphene oxide (NGO) layer (≈120 nm) on the Zn metal surface and named NGO@Zn, as shown in the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of Fig. 4 a [20]. In this architecture, abundant functional groups on the surface of NGO could increase the wettability of the electrode and electrolyte. The DFT showed that the CO and pyrrolic N (Npr) on the GO had a strong capture ability for the Zn atom because of their high binding energies of -0.24 and -0.46 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b). The easy-to-implement defect engineering of GO was beneficial to the diffusion and absorption of ions, and the doping of negatively charged heteroatoms enhanced the uniform Zn deposition. Benefit from the low lattice mismatch and the uniform graphene layer with zincophilic of the N-doped groups, which facilitated the directional deposition of Zn metal on the (002) plane, a dense and smooth surface was observed even after 100 cycles (Fig. 4c). As a result, the NGO@Zn electrode delivered an excellent Zn plating/stripping behavior (1200 h at 1.0 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2 and 300 h at 5.0 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2, respectively). As a typical 0D carbon material, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have the advantages of adjustable surface functional groups, strong chemical inertness, low toxicity, and low cost. Meanwhile, the surface passivation and functionalization of the CDs can regulate their physicochemical properties [90,91]. Therefore, Zhao et al. proposed high-yield CDs with abundant polar functional group (-CHO and -CN) as the Zn anode protection layer [92]. The CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal reaction of p-phenyldiacetonitrile and terephthalaldehyde in NaOH solution. The DFT calculation showed the strong binding ability of the Zn ion to -CHO and -CN, indicating that the abundant polar functional group could increase nucleation sites, reduce the nucleation barrier, and improve the Zn affinity. Moreover, the COMSOL numerical simulation revealed that the CD interfacial layer could balance the electric field distribution on the Zn anode surface. Therefore, the Zn plating processes are summarized in Fig. 4d. The CDs-coated Zn anode could not only isolate water molecules and repel SO4 2− anions but also increase the zincphilic sites and reduce the nucleation barrier, thus resulting in a planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition. Fig. 4e and f show the optical photograph and the tranmisson electron microscope (TEM) image of the obtained CDs, respectively. The high-quality CDs represented that the method could achieve large-scale production.

Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), a 2D atomic crystal, can effectively inhibit dendrite formation during Li plating because of its excellent chemical stability, high flexibility, and strong mechanical strength (Young's modulus about 1.0 TPa) of a single atomic layer [93,94] Meanwhile, the inherent electronic insulating properties of the BN determine its potential as an artificial interfacial layer on metal electrode surfaces. For example, Yan et al. demonstrated the direct growth of an ultrathin h-BN on the copper (Cu) current collector and achieved uniform Li metal deposition between the NB and Cu interlayers [95]. Xie et al. found that the chemical functionalization at defect sites could further enhance the chemical and mechanical properties of h-BN, and then proposed selective atomic layer deposition of LiF on h-BN to achieve a stable Li metal interface [96]. These studies provide valuable experience for the protection of Zn metal anode by BN as an interfacial layer. Meanwhile, as an analog of graphene, the research interest in the directional Zn deposition has been attracted. For example, Jia et al. successfully coated h-BN on the Zn metal surface (BN@Zn) by magnetron sputtering technique, in which the interface layer could effectively adjust the Zn ion concentration gradient and electric field distribution on the electrode surface, achieving the (002) preferred orientation Zn deposition [97]. The DFT calculation indicated that the BN on the Zn anode could enhance the Zn adsorption energy, thereby inducing a homogeneous Zn-ion flux. Meanwhile, the Zn ions adsorbed on the BN interface increased the electrode surface charge concentration and balanced the concentration gradient on the BN@Zn electrode, which is beneficial to the directional Zn deposition (Fig. 4g).

MXenes are a typical family of 2D transition metal carbides or carbonitrides. Owing to their good metallic electrical conductivity (∼104 S cm−1) and rich surface functional groups, they have been wildly used as a functionalized material in electrochemical energy storage applications[98–101]. Meanwhile, the outstanding mechanical strength, hydrophilicity, and flexibility also make it a promising artificial interface material in metal anodes [102–104]. Moreover, the (000l) crystal plane of MXene and the (002) crystal plane of Zn are the hexagonal arrangement structure, which provides the condition for achieving uniform Zn deposition [28,105]. Therefore, Zhang et al. adopted an in-situ assembly strategy to assemble ultrathin, thickness-controllable, and uniform MXene layers onto the Zn anode surface [43]. In this architecture, the MXene layer could effectively suppress the Zn dendrites and side reactions on the Zn anode. The COMSOL numerical simulation results revealed that the MXene layer could realize charge redistribution on the electrode surface, resulting in a lower nucleation energy barrier and a more uniformly distributed electric field for the Zn anode. Zhu et al. reported a zincophilic Ti3C2Tx MXene (MX-TMA) self-assembly interface layer on the Zn electrode for achieving dendrite-free Zn deposition [106]. The excellent hydrophilicity and compactness architecture of the MX-TMA layer not only enhanced the kinetics of Zn ion transport but also inhibited the side reactions at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Meanwhile, the low lattice mismatch (∼10%) between the (002) plane of the Zn and MXene promoted the Zn metal directional deposition along the (002) plane. Fig. 4h displays the electric field distribution on the pristine Zn and the MX-TMA@Zn electrodes. A more uniform interfacial electric field was observed on the MX-TMA@Zn electrode. This indicates that the MX-TMA layer could balance the interfacial electric field to achieve uniform Zn deposition. The MX-TMA@Zn symmetric battery delivered a stable cycling at a severe depth of discharge (85%) at 10 mA cm−2 with an areal capacity of 10 mA cm−2 more than 450 h (Fig. 4i), exhibiting the distinguished electrochemical performance of the MX-TMA@Zn electrode. Moreover, Li et al. reported a molten salt method to prepare a series of MXenes with halogen (-Cl2, -Br2, and -I2) functional groups as an artificial layer [107]. The high lattice match between MXene and Zn deposits ensured that a heterogeneous interfacial region was formed during the early deposition stage, which could induce Zn metal deposition along the (002) plane and achieve a dendrites-free Zn anode. Taking Ti3C2Cl2 as an example, the guiding effect of -Cl terminations on Zn ions promoted the tiling of the deposited Zn metal on the Ti3C2Cl2 and formed a heterogeneous interface during the initial deposition stage (Fig. 4j). Subsequently, the initially deposited Zn acted as a nucleation seed layer to induce the uniform deposition of subsequent Zn atoms.

Compared with inorganic rigid interfacial layers, elastic polymer interfacial layers generally exhibit superior mechanical stability and can significantly improve the electrochemical performance of Zn anodes [108]. Certainly, these polymer materials also need to meet high ionic conductivity and selectivity, good adhesion to the Zn surface, and excellent stability in the aqueous electrolyte when used as the interface layer of the Zn anode [109]. Meanwhile, the monomer of the polymer should have abundant polar groups to interact with solvated metal ions, which helps to regulate the distribution and migration of active species at the interface.[110]

The introduction of brightener in the Zn electroplating field can form a homogeneous diffusion layer on the plating substrate, which elevates the electrochemical polarization and nucleation overpotential, thereby increasing the nucleation active sites and refining the grain size of the Zn deposition [111–113]. Therefore, inspired by brighteners, Zhao et al. proposed a multifunctional polymer interface layer composed of polyamide (PA) and zinc trifluoromethanesulfonate (Zn(OTf)2) [110]. Benefiting from the unique hydrogen bond network and strong coordination ability with metal ions of the PA layer, which not only acted as a brightener to achieve a uniform Zn nucleation but also effectively prevented the corrosion of Zn electrodes. As a result, this polymer interface layer enabled the Zn metal anode to achieve stable cycling for over 8000 h at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2. Undoubtedly, this study provides valuable experience for stable Zn deposition with polymers as interfacial layers for Zn metal anodes. For example, Jiao et al. designed an ion-selective polymer glue coating layer on the Zn electrode surface, which achieved 90% Zn utilization and long-term stable plating/stripping of the Zn metal anode (Fig. 5 a) [114]. The polymer glue was obtained by dissolving polyethylene oxide and lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide in an acetone and dichloromethane binary solvent. Meanwhile, the diffusion coefficient of Zn ions and water into the polymer glue layer was simulated from molecular dynamics. As shown in Fig. 5b, the fast Zn-ion migration and the low water diffusivity indicated that the prepared polymer glue coating layer had selective permeability. Therefore, the mechanism could be summarized as the polymer coating layer on the Zn electrode surface prevented the diffusion of water in the electrolyte but allowed the rapid Zn-ion migration, while the interfacial layer provided an excessive energy barrier for the lateral movement of Zn ions. Therefore, compared with the pristine Zn electrode, Zn ions generated more nucleation sites on the surface of the polymer-coated Zn electrode and finally realized the (002) plane directional Zn deposition. Chen et al. developed thin polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) interface layer to induce the deposited metal with a preferential Zn (002) crystallographic orientation by regulating interfacial ion diffusion and obtaining durable and dendrite-free ZMBs [115]. The deposition mechanism of PVA-modified Zn electrodes (PVA@Zn) is summarized in Fig. 5c. The PVA coating layer promoted the uniform adsorption of Zn ions on the electrode surface, thereby inducing uniform nucleation. The preferred (002) plane Zn deposition was achieved by the PVA coating layer, reducing the deposition energy and the OTf− anion-induced (002) plane synergistic effect. In addition, the formation of interfacial H2O intermolecular hydrogen bonds reduced the side reactions on the metal surface, thereby ensuring long-term stable Zn anode. More recently, Xie et al. introduced a spontaneous polymerization reaction of anthraquinonediazonium tetrafluoroborate to chemically graft a thin polyanthraquinone (PAQ) coating layer on the Zn surface [116]. The DFT calculation revealed that the PAQ layer had a repulsive effect on Zn atoms, which facilitated the diffusion of Zn ions through the surface, thereby avoiding concentration gradients on the electrode surface. Meanwhile, Fig. 5d and e display the electric field and Zn ion concentration field simulations of the pristine Zn and the Zn@PAQ electrodes. The results suggest that the Zn electrode with the PAQ coating layer can adjust the electric field distribution at the initial deposition and significantly reduce the ion concentration gradient on the electrode surface. Therefore, the PAQ interfacial layer could effectively inhibit the 2D diffusion of Zn ions, homogenize the electric field and concentration distribution, and further induce Zn metal deposition along the (002) plane and achieve a dendrites-free Zn anode.

Ferroelectric materials, as the interfacial layer of metal electrodes, can improve ion migration kinetics and achieve homogeneous metal-ion flux, so due to their ferroelectricity, they induce uniform and switchable electrical polarization and form an internal electric field on the electrode surface, thereby enabling the orderly migration of ions. Alternatively, the compressive stress on the interfacial layer during the electroplating process will generate an additional out-of-plane piezoelectric field in the ferroelectric material, thereby promoting ion migration [81,117-119]. Therefore, considering the poor self-built electrostatic field inside the ZMBs, the polarization domain of the self-built electric field of the battery is limited, which makes it difficult for the ZMBs to meet the fast charging capability, that is, the rapid ion supply capability is insufficient. Zou et al. reported a method to realize a directional polarization and nanopore-rich Zn anode coating layer by pre-polarized polyvinylidene fluoride-BaTiO3 composite ferroelectric materials (poled BTO) [81]. In this work, Zn ions with homogeneous distribution and fast migration kinetics were achieved at the electrode-electrolyte interface through the assistance of the “homo-channeling” effect and the “ion-pumping” effect. As a result, a dense and horizontally arranged Zn metal deposition was achieved even at ultrahigh rates. Similarly, Wang et al. introduced a polarized ferroelectric polymer material (poly(vinylidene fluoride-trifluoroethylene) (P(VDF-TrFE))) as the Zn anode coating layer. The polarization direction of the P(VDF-TrFE) was regulated by pre-polarization treatment to generate a negative dipole center at the electrolyte-side surface, thereby forming an inner electric field at the interface. The polymer coating surface exhibited localized concentrations of Zn ions that promoted the deposited Zn horizontal growth [119]. As shown in Fig. 5f, the positive and negative bias voltages were applied on the different regions of the P(VDF-TrFE) surface, which showed obvious phase contrast, suggesting that the P(VDF-TrFE) coating has two kinds of polarization states. Combined with finite element simulations (Fig. 5g), the Zn ions were more likely to concentrate at the tip of the Zn protrusion in the upward polarized and non-polarized coating layers, and this trend was more serious in the upward polarized coating layer. In remarkable contrast, for the downward polarized coating layer, the concentration of Zn ions was mainly concentrated in the corners of the polymer and Zn protrusion. Fig. 5h-j represent the SEM images of P(VDF-TrFE)-coated Zn anodes after 1.0 mAh cm−2 metal deposition in three polarization states, respectively. It was clearly found that the Zn anode under the protection of the P(VDF-TrFE) coating layer with the downward polarization exhibited a more homogeneous deposition layer, indicating that the newly deposited Zn metal grew in a horizontal arrangement.

The slow evolution of the Li-ion solvation sheath increased the charge transfer and ion diffusion energy barriers, which led to Li anode degradation and CE declination in Li metal batteries using typical diluent electrolytes [120–122]. Similarly, a solvated Zn ion for desolvation and deposition also suffers a high energy barrier. This is because the close ion pairs [Zn(H2O)6]2+ formed by a Zn ion and six H2O have high charge density [33,123,124]. Meanwhile, the hydroxyl ions from water decomposition drive the formation of Zn ion-insulation by-product on the Zn anode, reducing the Zn utilization and aggravating dendrite formation [123,125,126]. Previous studies confirmed that the fluorine (F) atom termination mechanism could guide anatase TiO2 to expose a specific crystal plane. Benefiting from the lowest surface energy of the (001) plane terminated by the F atom, the exposed TiO2 plane was transformed from an intrinsically thermodynamically stable (101) plane to a more active (001) plane [67,127]. Therefore, Zhao et al. developed a fluorinated covalent organic framework (FCOF) film with a thickness of ∼100 nm via a solvothermal method as a protective layer to achieve a high stable Zn anode [68]. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the solvated Zn ions gradually approached the electrode driven by the electric field force, and then the water molecules coordinated with Zn ions were excluded from the surface of the FCOF film due to the strong hydrophobicity of the F element, thus achieving an effect of desolvation. Meanwhile, the fluorinated 1D ordered channels supplied regular ionic coordination sites, and hence realized the fast Zn ion transport. The first-principles calculations showed that the Zn (002) plane terminated by F atoms had lower surface energy and higher adsorption energy than the (101) plane. As a result, the FCOF film-protected Zn anode enhanced the deposition Zn horizontal growth along the (002) crystal plane and displayed a uniform Zn platting. In addition, the texture information of the deposition Zn was identified by the XRD pole. The (002) pole figure of the pristine Zn (Fig. 6b) showed a uniform distribution of the diffracted intensity along the radial direction, which indicated that the deposited Zn had a scattered crystal orientation without preferred crystal planes. In contrast, the diffracted intensity of the FCOF film protective Zn electrode was mainly concentrated at ψ = 0-20o (Fig. 6c), which implied the (002) crystal plane preference under the control mechanism of the FCOF film. Jian et al. prepared a sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone) (SPEEK) ultrathin interface layer on the Zn anode surface, which could block both water molecules and anions, thereby promoting Zn-ion desolvation and uniform Zn ion flow [109]. As shown in Fig. 6d, the SPEEK coating layer provided a proper ion channel size, which promoted the Zn-ion desolvation and blocked the anions and water, thereby suppressing side reactions on the Zn anode surface. Meanwhile, the SPEEK coating layer with uniform ion channels and good hydrophilicity could promote the 2D transport of Zn ions on the Zn anode and finally achieve long-term stable Zn deposition.

In addition, Zhang et al. constructed a cellulose nanowhiskers (CNWs)-graphene film (CNG) constructed with graphene and CNWs by CH-π interaction, which delivered a long-term stable cycling, displaying an excellent reversible Zn plating/stripping when employed as the interface layer on Zn anode (CNG/Zn) [33]. The DFT calculations showed that the graphene and CNWs had almost similar adsorption energy for Zn ions, while the CNWs displayed lower adsorption energy for H2O molecules (-0.31 eV) than that of graphene (-0.032 eV). Meanwhile, the molecular dynamics simulation indicated that the CNG attracts [Zn(H2O)6]2+ and then removed H2O molecules through hydrogen bonding interaction. Therefore, the mechanism of this interfacial layer could be summarized. The CNG interface layer could make the [Zn(H2O)6]2+ desolvation by the attraction of CNW and the rejection of GN to H2O, avoiding the Zn corrosion. In addition, the negative surface charge could generate a deanionization shock, which enhanced cations to pass through the interface layer and realized Zn deposition along with the Zn (002) plane (Fig. 6e). Qiu et al. reported a sulfonate group-functionalized BN (S-BN) nanosheets by the sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate-assisted liquid-phase exfoliation technique and used the spray coating method to modify the Zn anode (S-BN@Zn) [128]. The deposition mechanism of S-BN@Zn is summarized in Fig. 6f. The self-assembled structure of S-BN nanosheets divided the electrode-electrolyte interface into multiple independent pores, which was beneficial to the homogenization of Zn-ion flux in each confined space. Meanwhile, the sulfonate group on the surface of S-BN reduced the Zn-ion desolvation energy, thereby improving the Zn-ion migration kinetics and inducing a preferred Zn (002) plane growth.

Although the as-prepared interface layer obtained by the ex situ coating method has advantages in the performance adjustment and structural tailoring [85], it is usually hard to close contact with the electrode, resulting in side reactions still occurring on the Zn metal surface. Meanwhile, the weak adhesion makes the coating layer easily detached from the electrode during plating/stripping, thereby exposing the Zn metal directly to the electrolyte, which leads to the corrosion of the fresh metal. Compared with Li/Na metal, Zn metal has better environmental compatibility [11,129,130], which makes Zn anode easier to obtain interfacial layers by chemical/electrochemical processes. This interfacial layer obtained by an in situ strategy also exhibits a stable and dense architecture to satisfy the plating/stripping of Zn ions, thus having production scalability.[36]

As shown in Fig. 7 a, Hao et al. constructed a uniform ZnS interphase on the Zn substrate by an in situ vapor-solid strategy to realize the effective suppression of side reactions [47]. During the preparation of the ZnS@Zn electrode, the RTC of the Zn (002) lattice plane increased with increasing the temperature, which suggested that the formation of the preferred crystal planes on the Zn metal surface might be related to temperature. Meanwhile, according to the fact that the ZnS (002) surface was transformed from the Zn (002) (Fig. 7b) [131], the theoretical calculations revealed that the chemical bond between S and Zn atoms in Zn metal led to the uneven distribution of interfacial charges. The unbalanced charge distribution increased the diffusion kinetics of Zn ions in the interfacial layer and enhanced the stability of the ZnS layer on the Zn metal surface, which achieved a uniform Zn deposition. Meanwhile, this study provided an important reference for an in-depth understanding of Zn anode behavior in aqueous ZMBs and the design of long-life ZMBs.

Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is a method to produce a highly uniform and dense thin film on the substrate through chemical reactions of precursors at high temperatures. Inspired by its achievements in the protection of Li/Na metal batteries [132–136], it has also been involved in the field of ZMBs in recent years [30,41], including the construction interface layer on the Zn substrate [47,137] Clearly, to achieve the Zn (002) plane-directed growth of the deposited Zn, a low lattice mismatch (≤25%) between the substrate and the coating layer is necessary to be considered. Therefore, Yang et al. fabricated a ZnSe layer by in situ selenization of Zn substrate surfaces via a CVD method and obtained the heterostructure ZnSe@Zn with Zn metal. They successfully revealed the regulation mechanism of the preferred Zn (002) plane by the ZnSe coating layer on the Zn metal surface [138]. In this architecture, the ZnSe (111) crystal plane showed a low lattice mismatch of ≈13% with the Zn (002) crystal plane, implying that the ZnSe coating layer could guide the deposition of Zn atoms along the (002) plane (Fig. 7c). No obvious by-product peaks were found in the XRD pattern of the ZnSe-coated Zn electrode after cycling, which indicates that the coating layer could also effectively suppress the HER on the electrode interfaces. Meanwhile, the ZnSe-coated Zn electrode showed higher RTC of the Zn (002) plane than the pristine Zn electrode (Fig. 7d). This also confirmed that the ZnSe coating had the effect of guiding the deposition of Zn atoms along with the Zn (002) plane. The COMSOL multiphysics suggested that the in situ selenization could eliminate the protrudes on the pristine Zn substrate surface, thereby eliminating the tip effect and promoting the homogenization of the electric field on the Zn surface, enabling uniform Zn deposition (Fig. 7e). Lu et al. successfully coated a nanothickness AgZn3 on the Zn metal surface by a plasma sputtering technique to achieve a dense and dendrite-free Zn deposition [139]. Compared with the AgZn3 layer obtained by electrodeposition, the AgZn3 coating obtained by the plasma sputtering method could effectively avoid the slow alloying reaction rate of Ag-Zn in the initial state of deposition [38], thereby avoiding the preferential growth of dendrites. Meanwhile, benefiting from the good lattice matching between the AgZn3 (002) and Zn (002) planes, the directional Zn epitaxial deposition was achieved.

Conversion coating is a type of protective layer chemically bonded to the substrate produced by chemical or electrochemical treatment of the metal surface, which acts as an insulating barrier and/or corrosion inhibitor on the metal surface to protect the substrate [140]. Meanwhile, conversion coating as a mature technology has been widely used in the metal anti-corrosion field [141–143]. Recently, Deng et al. immersed Zn foil in a conversion solution containing Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, H3BO3, and H2O2, and finally obtained a cerium-based conversion film (CCF) on the Zn metal surface (Fig. 7f) [144]. The blessing of the CCF improved the corrosion resistance of the Zn metal grain boundaries (Fig. 7g), and its affinity for Zn ions reduced the nucleation barrier of Zn ions, resulting in the Zn deposition with the preferred Zn (002) plane.

Even though the inorganic coating layers play a positive role in promoting the uniform diffusion of Zn ions, the inherent fragility of inorganic interfacial layers is also a challenge. Sometimes the solvated Zn ions inevitably pass through the inorganic protective layer and contact the Zn substrate, resulting in the potential risk of corrosion the Zn anode. In contrast, the organic coating layer usually exhibits good flexibility and density to cope with volume change and desolvation during Zn deposition, while the relatively low mechanical modulus and poor ionic conductivity remain a concern. For the above reasons, Di et al. proposed an in-situ organic-inorganic hybrid coating layer on Zn anode surface by electrochemically pre-cycling Zn electrodes in a pre-configured solution (1.0 M Zn(OTF)2) in triethyl phosphate (TEP) solvent) [145]. As shown in Fig. 7h, the prepared hybrid coating layer displayed a two-layer structure, in which an inorganic Zn3(PO4)2-ZnF2-ZnS layer was covered by an organic layer. Compared with the pristine Zn anode, the organic-inorganic hybrid layer-coated Zn anode could enhance the ability to resist corrosion, H2 evolution, and dendrite growth. More importantly, for the protected Zn anode, the XRD diffraction peak intensity ratio of Zn (002) to Zn (100) displayed a marked increase from initial 1.46 to 4.91 (Fig. 7i). Meanwhile, the organic-inorganic hybrid layer-coated Zn electrode showed higher RTC of the Zn (002) lattice plane than that of the pristine Zn electrode after 50 cycles, which indicated that the deposited Zn atoms had a (002) preferred orientation. The reason for this result may be the synergistic mechanism of the organic-inorganic hybrid layer, in which the inorganic protective layer achieved a uniform distribution of Zn-ion flux, and the Zn2+-TEP and Zn2+-OTF− had smaller lowest unoccupied molecular orbital energies than free solvent and anion. Similarly, Wu et al. designed a 3D stacked lamellar matrix (SLM) composed of ZnF2/Zn3(PO4)2/CFX (x = 1 or 3) on the Zn anode, which was fabricated by electrochemical pretreatment Zn electrodes in 0.5 M Zn(OTF)2-TEP solvent (SLM-Zn) [146]. The XRD diffraction peak intensity ratio of Zn (002) to Zn (100) of the SLM-Zn electrode was higher than that of the pristine Zn electrode, implying that the pretreatment process can make the Zn electrode with a more exposed (002) plane. Meanwhile, the DFT calculation revealed that the SLM layer could reduce the Zn-ion desolvation energy, and the ZnF2 in the SLM layer was beneficial to improving the Zn-ion migration kinetics. Moreover, the strong interaction between the in situ formed SLM and the substrate hindered the lateral migration of Zn ions on the electrode surface, thereby regulating the Zn-ion flux and achieving uniform deposition (Fig. 7j). Wang et al. introduced a U-shaped planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition by in situ self-template constructing a 2D metal-organic framework nanoarray on a Zn anode. The Zn foil was first immersed into a solution of tetra-(4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP) ligand and dimethylformamide/ethanol solvent, and subjected to a low-temperature solvothermal process for several minutes. During this process, the TCPP tended to adsorb vertically on the Zn foil surface. The Zn ions oxidized by TCPP on the surface of the Zn foil would immediately coordinate and assemble with the TCPP and form the Zn-TCPP nanoarrays on the Zn foil [147]. As shown in Fig. 7k, the abundant zincphilic N- and O-containing sites on the TCPP nanoarrays uniformly pre-seed Zn ions. In the subsequent deposition process, the Zn ion started bottom-up lateral Zn deposition from the anode surface and finally achieved planar and dendrite-free U-shaped Zn deposition.

During the continuous plating process, the homogeneous nucleation of Zn ions at the electrode-electrolyte interface can achieve uniform Zn deposition, thereby effectively improving the electrochemical stability of the Zn metal anode. Recently, strategies that enable the electrode-electrolyte interface with a lower Zn nucleation energy barrier and more nucleation sites by introducing zincphilic metal materials to achieve long-term stable Zn metal deposition have been extensively studied. For example, Cui et al. employed the ion beam sputtering to anchor nano-gold (Au) on the surface of Zn electrodes and it serves as nucleation seeds for Zn deposition [148]. Benefiting from the guiding effect and strong attraction of nano-Au particles to Zn ions, the deposited Zn metal formed a uniform Zn flake array on the anode and exhibited a stable plating/stripping over 2000 h. Chen et al. reported a method to fabricate a flexible dendrite-free ZMB anode by inkjet printing silver (Ag) nanoparticles on 3D carbon cloth [149]. In this work, Ag nanoparticles act as deposition sites for Zn metal, inducing the ordered Zn deposition. Meanwhile, the strong binding energy between Ag and Zn atoms was beneficial to reducing the Zn nucleation overpotential and promoting the reversible cycling of the Zn electrode. Although these strategies were outstanding in improving the long-term stability of Zn electrodes, the modulation of the exposed crystal planes of Zn metal to achieve planar and dendrite-free Zn electrodes was still understood.

Tin (Sn) metal has a higher reduction potential than Zn, which allows it to be generated on the surface of the Zn electrode by a chemical displacement reaction. Meanwhile, considering that the alloying reaction of Zn and Sn is difficult to occur at room temperature, it is beneficial to analyze the role of Sn metal in the Zn deposition process from the perspective of crystallography. Therefore, Li et al. used a chemical replacement reaction to realize the Sn metal layer with different exposed crystal planes (Sn (101) and Sn (200) planes) on the Zn electrode surface by controlling the immersion time of Zn metal in SnCl4 solution [150]. DFT calculation showed that the binding energy of Zn atoms on different Sn crystal planes was smaller than that of Zn atoms on the Zn electrode. Meanwhile, calculation results of the surface energy of the Zn and Sn planes showed that the Sn (200) plane had higher surface energy. These results suggest that the Sn metal layer can significantly reduce the deposition energy barrier and induce a strong capillary action on the electrode surface. Moreover, combined with the electrode morphologies after Zn deposition, it could be found that the Zn electrode with Sn (200) crystal plane was conducive to the lateral growth of the newly deposited Zn metal. Similarly, Zhai et al. reported a stable Zn anode with a preferred Sn (200) plane by a flame normalizing process, which involved soaking a commercial Zn foil into a 0.1 M SnCl4 solution for 5 min and then uniformly heating the Zn foil with a butane torch for 10 s [151]. Compared to the pristine Zn electrode, the more exposed Sn (200) surface-modified Zn electrode exhibited better resistance to side reactions, wettability, and lower nucleation/deposition energy barriers.

Recently, the introduction of metallic Cu to construct the Zn anode interface layer for realizing a stable Zn metal anode has been widely reported. There are some advantages of metallic Cu as the interface layer of the Zn electrode. First, the low cost and chemical inertness of metallic Cu can improve the corrosion resistance of the Zn electrode. In addition, the zincphilic Cu sites in the Cu-Zn alloys can significantly enhance the adsorption of Zn ions and promote uniform Zn nucleation on the electrode surface. For example, Cai et al. introduced an anti-corrosive Zn electrode with Cu/Zn composite interface, which was obtained by immersing Zn foil in CuCl2 solution and then heat treatment in Ar/H2 atmosphere was followed [23]. Zhou et al. proposed a method to realize a highly reversible Zn electrode by directly pre-depositing Zn metal on a Cu-Zn alloy substrate (CZ-Zn) [152]. Compared with the pristine Zn electrode, the strong interaction of Zn ions with Cu-Zn alloy endowed the CZ-Zn electrode with highly reversible Zn plating/stripping behavior. Meanwhile, the inherent inertness of metallic Cu to HER and the high dezincification potential of Cu-Zn alloy could effectively suppress the side reactions on the electrode surface, which further improved the electrochemical stability of the CZ-Zn electrode. Zeng et al. designed a 3D multifunctional Zn metal anode, which utilized an electrospinning process to anchor Cu nanoboxes on N-doped carbon fibers (Cu NBs@NCF), and achieved stable Zn plating/stripping (Fig. 8 a) [153]. Fig. 8b displays the elemental mapping images of Cu NBs@NCFs, which implied that Cu, C, and N elements were uniformly distributed on the carbon fiber. The XRD detected CuZn5 diffraction peaks on the NBs@NCFs electrode after Zn plating, which suggests the formation of Cu-Zn alloy and solid solution interface. Meanwhile, the DFT calculation showed that the Zn atom had stronger binding energy to both Cu and Cu-Zn alloy, which indicated that Cu and CuZn5 could lower the energy barrier of Zn nucleation and form homogeneous Zn deposition. As a result, the Cu NBs@NCF anode after Zn plating displayed a more exposed (002) plane and a smooth surface morphology. Meng et al. proposed a 3D nanoporous Zn electrode with a ZnxCuy alloy shell, which was obtained by an anionic surfactant-assisted in situ alloying reaction of Cu and Zn [154]. Fig. 8c displays the schematic diagram of the nanoporous ZnxCuy/Zn electrode. First, the nanoporous Zn precursor with a preferred (002) plane was obtained by dealloying an alloy of Zn50Al50, followed by selective etching in an N2‑purged KOH solution. Subsequently, the Cu nanoparticles were homogeneously anchored on the porous Zn electrode surface through the displacement reaction in a CuCl2 solution. The sodium dodecyl sulfate molecules adsorbed on the electrode surface could isolate the free water molecules on the electrode/electrolyte interface, which promoted the in situ surface alloying of Cu and Zn and finally formed a ZnxCuy alloy shell on the nanoporous Zn electrode. The DFT calculation showed that the Zn atom had lower adsorption energy on the ZnxCuy (100) plane than that on the Zn (002) plane, and the adsorption energy of the Zn at the edge site was lower than that at the top site. This result implies that the ZnxCuy interface is more favorable for the homogeneous epitaxial growth of the Zn metal. As a consequence, the nanoporous ZnxCuy/Zn electrode exhibited long-term dendrite‑free Zn deposition.

The strategies to achieve dendrite-free Li deposition by controlling the preferred crystal planes of Cu current collectors have been successfully demonstrated in recent years [155–157]. Therefore, Yan et al. systematically expounded the mechanism of the Cu (100) plane-induced (002) plane directional Zn deposition from the perspective of atomic structure [158]. Theoretical simulations showed that the initial deposition of the Zn atom onto the Cu (100) electrode was underpotential deposition (UPD). The atomic arrangement of this deposited layer was closer to the face-centered cubic structure. Subsequent deposition of the Zn atom onto the initial deposition layer was transformed into overpotential deposition (OPD) with an hcp structure. Fig. 8d shows the schematic diagram of the crystal orientation of the Zn UPD and OPD layers on the Cu (100). Meanwhile, the XRD pole figures of Zn deposited on a Cu (100) electrode showed that the Zn (002) plane (Fig. 8e) exhibited a sharper intensity than the Zn (101) plane (Fig. 8f), indicating that the deposited Zn metal has a preferred (002) orientation. Because the first Zn deposition layer had a similar crystal structure to that of the Cu (100) plane, the Cu (100) and the Zn (002) planes had a low lattice mismatch, thereby realizing a planar dendrite-free Zn deposition with a high CE of 98.85% (Fig. 8g).

Evidently, in the strategies of constructing an artificial interface layer to realize Zn metal directional deposition, some influencing factors also need to be considered on the premise that the interface layer satisfies the characteristics of the ideal artificial interface layer. For example, from the crystallographic point of view, the interface layer and the Zn (002) plane need to be lattice-matched and compatible. From the perspective of chemical energy, it is necessary to realize the lower surface energy and higher adsorption energy of the (002) plane under the control of the interface layer to improve the competitive advantage.

As a significant part of the battery, the electrolyte acts as an ionic conductor to provide ionic current between the two electrodes, which plays a decisive role in the electrochemical performance of batteries. Meanwhile, the working voltage of the battery is determined by the potential difference of the redox reaction between the electrode materials, and the potential difference should be within the electrochemical stability window of the electrolyte, that is, the voltage window of the HER and oxygen evolution reaction [159–161]. Although aqueous electrolyte has the advantages of cost-effectiveness, intrinsic safety, and high ionic conductivity [10,12], the window of traditional aqueous electrolyte is no more than 2.0 V (for pure water, it is 1.23 V) [161], which makes ZMBs face the challenge of insufficient energy density. Moreover, the strong solvated Zn ions, the high energy barrier required for desolvation, and some accompanying side reactions (Zn corrosion, surface passivation, and H2 evolution) in ZMBs due to thermodynamically unstable Zn metal in aqueous solutions hinder their large-scale application. Therefore, choosing a suitable electrolyte is important for realizing high-performance ZMBs, and the following aspects should be considered: (1) economic feasibility, environmental friendliness, and excellent ionic conductivity; (2) a wide electrochemical window and an acceptable pH to meet the redox reaction of electrode materials; (3) good compatibility with battery components, including electrode materials, separator, and current collectors; (4) excellent thermal stability to avoid thermal runaway during high power operation. Considering that the undesirable reactions of Zn anode in mild media are weakened [162,163], the two electrolyte systems of zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) and Zn(OTf)2 were more concerned by researchers in recent years. However, further exploration of state-of-the-art electrolytes is required to achieve long-term stable directional Zn deposition, especially against high current density and areal capacity test conditions.

Although the ZnSO4 is one of the most widely used electrolytes in ZMBs, it is difficult to realize the growth of Zn metal along the (002) plane on the commercial Zn foil, individually, because it prefers the (101) orientation. Therefore, the introduction of additives as one strategy of electrolyte optimization has been considered a promising approach to achieving directional Zn deposition [164,165]. For example, Sun et al. investigated the regulation of the newly deposited Zn metal exposed crystal plane by additives such as cetyltrimethylammonium bromide, sodium dodecyl sulfate, polyethylene-glycol, and thiourea. The results showed that in the ZnSO4-based electrolyte, polyethylene glycol as an additive made the deposition Zn have more excellent corrosion resistance and dendrite suppression, which is mainly attributed to the exposure of more (002) and (103) planes [166]. Furthermore, Feng et al. achieved the solvated structure modulation of Zn ions and induced the Zn deposition along the (002) plane by introducing dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) into the ZnSO4 electrolyte, thereby suppressing side reactions and dendrite formation [167]. In detail, the DFT calculation results showed that the binding energy of Zn ions and DMSO was higher than that of Zn ions and H2O molecules. Therefore, the H2O molecules in the Zn-ion solvation shell would be replaced by DMSO to form [Zn(H2O)m(DMSO)n]2+ structure (Fig. 9 a). As shown in Fig. 9b, the newly deposited Zn metal could achieve a hexagonal layered epitaxial deposition when employed by the DMSO (with a volume ratio of 20%) modified electrolyte. The XRD results displayed that the RTC of the Zn (002) plane also increased with the increase of cycle, implying the deposition of Zn metal along the (002) plane. Meanwhile, the higher adsorption energy of the Zn (002) plane to DMSO than that to H2O indicates that the DMSO additive could increase corrosion resistance and inhibit dendrite growth (Fig. 9c). The special structure of citrate anions (Cit3−) in trisodium citrate (TSC) provided conditions for metal-ion complexation according to the earlier studies, so smooth electroplating surfaces could be obtained when TSC was employed as a complexing agent [168–170]. Therefore, Wang et al. used the TSC as an electrolyte additive to evaluate its contribution to the stability of Zn metal plating/stripping [54]. The different coordination environments of Zn ions in ZnSO4 and ZnSO4+TSC electrolytes were confirmed by X-ray absorption near edge structures. Then, the Zn-ion solvation structure was optimized by combing the DFT and the X-ray absorption fine structure curve fitting. After that, the solvation structure of Zn ion in ZnSO4+TSC electrolyte was determined as [ZnCit(H2O)3]− (Fig. 9d, and e). Different from the dendrite and corrosion problems faced by Zn anode in ZnSO4 electrolyte (Fig. 9f), in ZnSO4+TSC electrolyte, the [ZnCit(H2O)3]− structure had a passivation effect on water molecules, which could suppress side reactions on the Zn metal surface. In addition, the aggregation of Cit3− on the electrode surface and the strong coordination between Cit3− and Zn ions during the deposition process enabled homogeneous Zn-ion flux and directional Zn deposition (Fig. 9g). Meanwhile, sorbitol (SBT) as an electroplating solution additive has been proved to inhibit the growth of dendrites in the experiment of depositing lead, which is attributed to the addition of SBT making the alkaline plating solutions more stable [171,172]. Recently, Qiu et al. introduced the SBT as an additive in ZnSO4 electrolyte to obtain the preferred Zn (002) orientation deposition [70]. By comparing the pole diagrams of the (002) crystal plane after the deposition under different conditions, it could be found that the deposited Zn metal exhibited a more concentrated (002) crystal plane distribution in the SBT-ZnSO4 electrolyte system than that in the ZnSO4 electrolyte (Fig. 9h). The molecular dynamics simulations revealed that the Zn-ion solvation sheath was formed by 5 H2O molecules and 1 STB molecule (Zn2+-SBT). In this architecture, the SBT strongly interacted with H2O to form new hydrogen bond networks which broke the original structure in the pristine electrolyte, thereby avoiding side reactions on the Zn anode. Moreover, DFT calculations displayed the absorption energy of Zn2+-SBT on different Zn planes (Fig. 9i). The result showed the highest absorption energy of Zn2+-SBT on the Zn (002) plane. This suggests the more exposed Zn (002) plane and the higher RTC of the Zn (002). In addition, Shang et al. reported a propylene glycol (PG) as an additive for achieving stable Zn anode cycling [164]. By comparing the XRD of the deposition Zn metal under different concentrations of PG additives, it could be found that PG additive had the ability to realize the deposited Zn metal along with the (002) plane. Meanwhile, this work also reminds us that the concentration of electrolyte additives also affected the morphology of the deposited metal. This is because the high concentration of additives sacrifices the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte and may lead to electrode polarization, thereby reducing the electrochemical performance of the ZMBs.[162]

Therefore, some information can be summarized from the above optimization of the ZnSO4-based liquid electrolyte to realize the directional Zn deposition. First, the structural design of the Zn-ion solvation sheath plays a significant role in suppressing side reactions on the Zn electrode surface and achieving long-term stable Zn deposition. It can be roughly summarized as strong coordination and partial replacement of water molecules between the additives and Zn ions, thereby inhibiting the decomposition of water molecules and slowing down the occurrence of side reactions. Second, from the perspective of binding energy, the additive reduces the surface energy of the Zn (002) crystal plane, which provides conditions for the directional deposition of Zn metal. However, it is not difficult to find that the Zn-ion solvation sheath structure becomes larger with the substitution of water molecules, which could lead to slow Zn-ion migration kinetics. The electrochemical performance of the corresponding symmetric battery shows an obvious voltage hysteresis phenomenon. Certainly, the ionic conductivity and viscosity of the electrolyte should also be considered. This is because the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte is usually positively correlated with ion diffusion in the battery system, and a high ionic conductivity feedbacks a lower voltage hysteresis [17,173-175]. Furthermore, the viscosity of the electrolyte increases with the additive concentration, and the additive may form a passivation layer on the metal surface, thereby exhibiting a voltage hysteresis phenomenon [162, 164, 176].

With the rapid development of wearable electronics in the past few years, the exploration and design of flexible power sources have attracted great attention [177–179]. Compared with traditional liquid electrolytes, the gel electrolytes formed by polymer have the ionic conductivity of liquid electrolytes and structural stability which endow the gel electrolyte-assembled power sources with more stable power output and safety performance [87,180,181]. Meanwhile, an ingenious design of gel electrolytes could promote the development of portable, flexible, and transparent electronics [182,183].

Recently, the strategies to design gel electrolytes for ZMBs have received much attention, as the gel electrolytes have been confirmed to be effective in suppressing Zn dendrite growth and side reactions [77,184,185]. Among them, the strategy of achieving directional deposition of the Zn (002) plane via gel electrolyte has also been mentioned, which undoubtedly drives the development of long-life flexible ZMBs. For example, Hao et al. reported a zwitterionic polymer poly(3-(1-vinyl-3-imidazolio) propanesulfonate) (PVIPS) gel electrolyte mechanically reinforced with bacterial cellulose that achieved homo-epitaxial Zn deposition by modulating the coordination of Zn ions [71]. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the charged groups (sulfonate (SO3 −) and imidazole groups) in PVIPS partially replace water molecules in the solvation sheath of Zn ion architecture to form R-SO3 − [Zn(H2O)4]2+ −SO3-R groups. Meanwhile, the DFT calculations confirmed that the synergistic effect of the SO3 − group and imidazole ring promoted the directional deposition of Zn ions along with the Zn (002) plane. Compared with the pristine ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte, the ZMBs assembled using this gel electrolyte could suppress dendrite growth and side reactions under high current (Fig. 10b and c). Similarly, Lin et al. designed a polyacrylamide-poly (ethylene glycol) diacrylate-carboxymethyl cellulose (PMC) gel electrolyte to obtain the flexible and stable ZMBs (Fig. 10d) [186]. In this study, the Zn-ion solvation structure in gel electrolyte was simplified to one amide group and five water molecules (PAM-Zn/5H2O). Considering that the deposition rate of Zn on the electrode surface was limited by the desolvation process of Zn ions, the activation energies at different temperatures were calculated by combining with the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test and the Arrhenius equation [31,35]. The results showed that the PMC gel electrolyte had a faster charge transport rate than the pristine ZnSO4 electrolyte, which also indicated that the amide groups in the PMC gel electrolyte facilitated the desolvation of Zn ions. In addition, the GIXRD showed that the diffraction peak of the Zn (002) plane became strong with the increase of the cycle time. This means that the Zn ion channel formed by the amide group in PMC gel electrolyte could induce the deposition of Zn metal toward the (002) plane direction. The SEM images of Zn anode under different electrolytes (Fig. 10e and f) after 200 h cycling showed that the ZMBs assembled with gel electrolyte could withstand long-term stable plating/stripping.

Undoubtedly, compared with liquid electrolytes, the advantages of the gel electrolyte can be summarized as: 1) the limited water content can delay side reactions on the electrode surface; 2) the relatively high modulus can inhibit the growth of Zn dendrites and improve the electrochemical performance of the ZMBs; 3) the unique flexibility and good mechanical stability provide the possibility for the development of wearable devices. However, the mechanism of reversible Zn deposition with the preferred (002) orientation using gel electrolytes is still unclear. The Hofmeister series showed that SO4 2− had a strong precipitation ability for conventional polymer, so it is a challenge to increase the concentration of ZnSO4 salts to regulate the Zn-ion solvation structure in gel electrolytes[187,188]. Furthermore, the relatively low ionic conductivity, the poor electrode/electrolyte interfacial interaction, and the resulting high overpotential still require further attention.

Compared with the ZnSO4 electrolyte, the Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte has been confirmed to maintain higher reversibility and faster kinetic during Zn plating/stripping due to the hydrophobic of the OTf− anions (vs. SO4 2−) which could lessen the number of water molecules around the Zn ion and reduce the solvation effect [17,162,189]. Sun et al. compared the hydrophobicity of the OTf−, bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (TFSI−) and SO4 2− by the quantum chemistry calculations [189]. The lowest electrostatic potential of the SO4 2− indicates that it is more easy to form a hydrogen bond with a water molecule. However, the OTf− and TFSI− showed a lower molecular polarity index, suggesting that OTf− and TFSI− anions had a great hydrophobicity. Previously, Dong et al. revealed the composition of the SEI on the Zn anode surface based on the Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte with the dimethyl carbonate (DMC) additive by the molecular dynamics simulation, DFT calculation, and spectroscopic analysis [190]. The result reveals that the Zn2+-DMC and Zn2+-OTf− suffer from reductive decomposition before Zn deposition and form an organic-inorganic hybrid interfacial layer with rich ZnF2 and ZnCO3 on the Zn anode surface. Subsequently, Li et al. found that the decomposition of the Zn2+-OTf− could form a protective layer containing organic CF3 and inorganic ZnF2 and ZnS on the surface of the Zn anode, and the ZnF2 layer was connected with the Zn anode directly [191]. It is worth noticing that the excellent ionic conductivity of the ZnF2 layer facilitates the redistribution of Zn-ion flux on the electrode surface. Meanwhile, the derived organic layer could prevent the direct contact of Zn metal with water, thus inhibiting the growth of Zn dendrites and side reactions [31,174,191].

Yuan et al. reported that the sulfonate anion in Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte coordination with Zn ion could induce newly deposited Zn metal with preferentially exposed (002) plane in a three-electrode system (Fig. 11 a) [52]. The molecular electrostatic potential map of the Zn(OTf)2(H2O)4 structure is shown in Fig. 11b. Meanwhile, the SEM image displayed that the hexagonal Zn flakes were arranged in parallel on the substrate after stripping/plating at 10.0 mA cm−2, indicating that the Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte was favorable for the planar Zn deposition (Fig. 11c). It is noticed that the trend towards Zn (002) deposition using Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte alone does not seem to be evident in the coin cell system [115]. This may be related to the battery system, electrolyte concentration, electrode quality, electrode distance, or even the use of the separator. In addition, the research on how to improve the Zn electrode plating/stripping CE and extend the cycle life of Zn anode based on Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte, especially in terms of high current density and areal capacity is also an urgent problem to be solved. Therefore, Ouyang et al. constructed the multifunctional indium (In) metal interface layer on the Zn substrate by adding In(OTf)3 to 3 M Zn(OTf)2 [65]. Fig. 11d shows the optical photographs of the Zn electrode after 10 and 100 cycles in the pristine and additive-modified electrolytes. In stark contrast to the Zn electrode with the original electrolyte, Zn metal exhibited a smoother surface in the In(OTf)3 modified electrolyte than that in pristine electrolyte. The DFT calculation indicated that the Zn atom on the In (002) plane had lower binding energy (Fig. 11e). Meanwhile, the low lattice mismatch (13.6%) between the Zn (002) and In (002) (Fig. 11f) was favorable for homo-epitaxial Zn deposition. The large electronic interaction promoted Zn ion adsorption on the In (002) plane during the Zn deposition, and then, under the regulation of the In (002) plane, the Zn ions grew preferentially on the (002) plane, resulting in a homogeneous Zn deposition (Fig. 11g). As a result, when the employed In(OTf)3 additive was 0.3 M, the Zn anode modified by the In metal interphase maintained a stable CE of 99.94% during a long-term (3296 h) plating/stripping, and the Zn symmetric battery could work more than 5700 h, continuously. Besides, Zhang et al. used a green and economical nettle extract (NE) as an additive to construct zincophilic seeds on the Zn anode, which improved the stability of the symmetric battery to 2200 h at 5.0 mA cm−2 for 5.0 mAh cm−2. Meanwhile, under the condition of 0.5 mA cm−2 for 1.0 mAh cm−2, the Zn anode still maintained a CE of 99.7% after 500 cycles of plating/stripping [192]. In addition, Ma et al. achieved a stable organic-inorganic hybrid interface by 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME) as an additive in Zn(OTF)2 electrolyte, which could maintain long-term stable Zn plating/stripping under high current densities and areal capacities [193]. As shown in Fig. 11h, the DFT calculation displayed that the adsorption energy of DME on the Zn (002) plane was smaller than that of water, indicating that the adsorption advantage of water on the Zn surface was weaker than DME. Meanwhile, the charge density difference implied a strong chemisorption effect between the DME and Zn (Fig. 11i). Furthermore, the in-depth XPS revealed a stable interface layer on the Zn anode, which was a dense architecture composed of ZnF2-ZnS encapsulated by an organic layer (Fig. 11j). This interface layer played a significant role in uniform Zn-ion flux and suppression of side reactions on the Zn metal surface.

Although the employed Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte in ZMBs shows better electrochemical performance than the ZnSO4 electrolyte, the large molecular weight and the high price of Zn(OTf)2 lead to higher cost for the same concentration of electrolyte [63]. Therefore, how to tailor low-concentration electrolytes for stable Zn deposition is an ideal strategy with economic benefits. To meet this trend, Cao et al. reported a stable Zn metal deposition by adding the polyethylene glycol (PEG) additive to a 1 M Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte to modulate the Zn-ion solvation sheath [69]. The Zn symmetric battery with the PEG-modified 1 M Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte delivered a smooth plating/stripping over 1500 h at 0.25 mA cm−2 for 0.125 mAh cm−2. Meanwhile, it also maintained a high average CE of 98.8% at 2.0 mA cm−2 for 1.0 mAh cm−2. At almost the same time, Wu et al. presented the PEG as a dilute Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte additive to reduce the activity of water molecules while obtaining a ZnF2-rich interface layer for smooth Zn deposition with a preferred (002) crystal plane [194]. As shown in Fig. 11k, with the PEG-modified electrolyte, the water molecules on the Zn metal surface would be replaced by anions and PEG molecules. Meanwhile, the OTf− could be decomposed and form a ZnF2-rich layer on the Zn anode. Therefore, the PEG-modified electrolyte made the Zn anode surface smoother than the electrolyte without PEG during the deposition process, thereby exhibiting stable plating/stripping.

Obviously, the ZMBs depend on the Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte which usually exhibits a higher CE and reversibility than the ZnSO4 electrolyte. The OTf− anions with low molecular polarity have strong hydrophobicity, which can reduce the coordination number of water molecules around the Zn ions, thereby endowing a low desolvation energy barrier. Meanwhile, the OTf− anions decompose and then form a ZnF2 layer on the Zn anode, which improves the Zn ion transport kinetics, resulting in the small electrode polarization. The sulfonate anion in Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte coordination with Zn ion can induce newly deposited Zn metal with a preferential Zn (002) plane, which is beneficial to realizing the planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition. In addition, due to the synergistic effect of Zn(OTf)2 and additives, the Zn metal anode can achieve a uniform and stable Zn deposition under high current density and areal capacity. However, similar to the ZnSO4-based electrolyte, the introduction of additive results in a large voltage hysteresis at the Zn anode. Meanwhile, the viscosity of the electrolyte increases with the additive concentration, and the high price of the Zn(OTf)2 salt needs to be considered.

It is a consensus that ZnSO4 and Zn(OTF)2 are the two most common mild aqueous electrolytes in ZMBs. However, exploration and optimization of other types of electrolytes to achieve more exposed (002) plane Zn deposition have also been reported.

For example, Jin et al. proposed a method to regulate the electro-crystallization of Zn by adding PEG to an aqueous solution containing Zn ions and halide (X = Cl, Br, and I) ions ((PEG-Zn2+-aX−) (a =1,2,3)) [195]. The inspiration came from the fact that halide ions could selectively adsorb on the surface of some metal particles (Au, Pt, Ag, etc.) to realize the regulation of the metal growth direction [196–199]. Comparing the SEM images of Zn deposition in different electrolyte systems, it could be found that the morphology in the ZnSO4 electrolyte was vertical hexagonal Zn plates, whereas in the ZnX2 electrolyte systems were a self-assembled sphere. The EIS and limiting current under different concentrations of electrolytes confirmed that the adsorption of the unstable ion complexes ([ZnX3]−, [ZnX2], and [ZnX]+ (X = Br, I)) at the electrode-electrolyte interface could significantly reduce the interface resistance. Meanwhile, the exchange current of the Zn halide salts was higher than that of the ZnSO4 electrolyte, which indicates that the unstable ion complexes have a lower desolvation. Furthermore, to enable the rapid and preferential adsorption of the unstable ionic complexes on the Zn (002) plane, polyethylene glycol with a molecular weight of 300 g mol−1 (PEG300) was chosen as the electrolyte additive. Fig. 12 a displays the XRD pattern of the Zn deposition in ZnI2 aqueous electrolyte with or without PEG300. A preferentially exposed (002) plane was detected when using the ZnI2 aqueous electrolyte with PEG300. Meanwhile, the RTC of the Zn (002) plane remained unchanged with the increase of the deposition time, implying the deposition of Zn metal along the (002) plane (Fig. 12b).

Yao et al. introduced the triethylmethylammonium chloride (TC) as a quaternary ammonium (TMA) cation additive to 0.5 M ZnCl2 electrolyte (TC-ZC) to evaluate its contribution to the smooth Zn plating/stripping [51]. The TC electrolyte additive could inhibit the dendrite growth and the by-product formation was confirmed by comparing the XRD patterns of cycled Zn electrodes and the surface morphology of the Zn electrodes after electrolyte immersion. Meanwhile, the DFT calculation results based on the Zn (002) plane showed that the TMA cations could be stably adsorbed on the Zn electrode surface, thus covering the protruded sites. Subsequently, the adsorbed TMA cations at the protruded sites would suppress the accumulation of Zn ions at the tip sites through the electrostatic repulsion, thereby homogenizing the Zn deposition (Fig. 12c). Yang et al. proposed economical nicotinamide (NAM) as an additive for ZnCl2 electrolyte and systematically revealed the mechanism of the NAM electrolyte additive in achieving a smooth and fine-grained Zn deposition on carbon fibers [200].
