Molecular dynamics simulations showed that the NAM additive could reshape the Zn-ion solvation structure and form a Zn2+-4H2O-NAM solvation shell by replacing two H2O molecules in the Zn2+-6H2O solvation structure, which facilitated the Zn-ion desolvation energy. The DFT calculation indicated that the Zn ion had lower binding with the NAM molecule. In addition, the NAM molecule also revealed lower adsorption energies on the Zn (002) plane than H2O, implying that the NAM molecule could preferentially adsorb on the electrode surface to induce planar Zn deposition and suppress various side reactions (Fig. 12d).

In addition, Cong et al. prepared a polyanionic hydrogel electrolyte (PAMPSZn) by ion exchange and free-radical polymerization for highly reversible Zn deposition with the preferred (002) orientation [201]. As shown in Fig. 12e, the zinc 2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propane sulfonate (AMPSZn) was first prepared by ion exchange between the 2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propane sulfonic acid (AMPS) and zinc carbonate. Then, the PAMPSZn was obtained by the free-radical polymerization of AMPSZn, N,N′-Methylenebis(acrylamide), and α-Ketoglutaric acid. The assembled Zn symmetric battery with PAMPSZn hydrogel electrolyte delivered an ultra-stable Zn plating/stripping with a low overpotential of 0.1 mV after 4500 h. Meanwhile, the GIXRD showed that the diffraction peak of the Zn (002) plane became strong with the increase of the cycle number. This suggests that the PAMPSZn hydrogel electrolyte can induce the deposition of Zn metal toward the (002) plane direction. Furthermore, Fig. 12f summarizes the Zn plating/stripping mechanism with ZnSO4 and PAMPSZn hydrogel electrolyte. The PAMPSZn hydrogel electrolyte could reduce the -SO3 − contact with the Zn metal anode, thereby reducing the concentration polarization of anions and suppressing side reactions. Meanwhile, the polyanionic chain could build a uniform Zn-ion channel on the surface of the Zn anode, which could redistribute the Zn-ion flux. As a result, ZMBs with the PAMPSZn hydrogel electrolyte exhibited high reversible electrochemical performance.

The separator, an electronically insulating porous membrane, functions in the battery system to prevent the short circuit from cathode and anode contacting and to provide a channel for ion transport, which is an indispensable part of the battery assembly. An ideal battery separator should meet (1) electronic insulation to prevent short circuits of electrodes; (2) excellent wettability and open channels for electrolyte infiltration and ion transport; (3) good stability and mechanical strength to avoid volume change of the separator and deformation caused by extrusion during battery assembly; (4) chemical and electrochemical stability against chemical reactions with electrodes/electrolytes; (5) good adhesion to substrate materials.

Among the commercial separators, glass fiber (GF) is mostly used as a separator for ZMBs, which displays low electrical conductivity, high ionic conductivity (∼17.3 mS cm−1) after intaking electrolytes, and good wetting behavior in aqueous electrolytes [30,202]. Nevertheless, the nonselective ion transport as well as the large and uneven pore structure of the common GF separators easily causes non-uniform ion concentration gradients on the Zn anode surface, which induces dendritic growth. In addition, the fragile nature and the low strength of GF are weak to resist the growth of dendrites, which can lead to battery failure [203–205]. Therefore, the design and modification of the GF separator is also a factor to be considered in realizing the long-term stability of metal batteries. For example, Li et al. used a plasma-enhanced CVD method to in situ grow a graphene layer on one side of a commercial GF separator, in which the O and N elements doped vertical graphene architecture showed an excellent zincophilicity ability, and the conductive 3D graphene networks could balance electric field distribution, resulting in a smooth Zn ion transport and hence achieving a highly stable Zn plating/stripping [30]. Zhi et al. found that the collagen hydrolysate (CH) displayed negative surface charge properties in aqueous electrolytes and enabled a deionization shock in charged porous channels under overlimiting current, so a CH modified glass mat (CH@AGM) separator was employed [206]. As shown in Fig. 13 a, due to the positively charged surface of the bare AGM, the reversed surface conduction suppressed the overlimiting current and caused a reduction in metal-ion flux. At the same time, the anionic counterions were far away from the electrode, which hindered the diffusion of metal ions and eventually led to the dendrite growth (the corresponding schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 13c). In contrast, as shown in Fig. 13b, the CH@AGM is negatively charged, and the positive surface conduction increases the overlimiting current. Meanwhile, the anionic counterions are further repelled to form a “deionization shock” and achieve uniform metal deposition (the corresponding schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 13d). Furthermore, the combination of CH and metal ions could further achieve the uniform ion distribution on the metal electrode surface and have the effect of guiding metal deposition in the horizontal direction.

Benefiting from the small lattice mismatch between graphene (002) and Zn (002) crystal planes, therefore, Zn deposition along with the (002) crystal plane direction can also be achieved by modifying the separator by a graphene. Cao et al. used the vacuum filtration method to prepare graphene oxide (GO) modified GF (GO-GF) separator [207]. The XRD analysis revealed that the GO-GF separator could adjust the deposition direction of Zn ions, indicating the preferential Zn (002) plane (Fig. 13e). Correspondingly, the Zn symmetric battery employed by the GO-GF separator could also achieve stable plating/stripping for 500 cycles at a current density of 20 mA cm−2. This implies that the graphene-based materials have great potential in the field of separator modification. More recently, considering the unicity of the above strategies in regulating the electrolyte-GF/separator-anode interface, Liang et al. used BaTiO3 with spontaneous polarization and zincophilicity to modify the GF separator (BTO-GF) [208]. The prepared BTO-GF separator not only accelerated the transport of Zn ions between the electrolyte and GF interface but also redistributed Zn ions through the separator-anode interface, realizing the preferred Zn (002) plane deposition (Fig. 13f). Benefiting from the regulation of Zn-ion flux by the BTO-GF separator, the Zn metal anode maintained a smooth dendrite-free surface after working for 200 h with the current density of 1.0 mA cm−2 and the areal capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2 (Fig. 13g).

Cellulose nanofibers, with low cost, good hydrophilicity, excellent mechanical strength, chemical stability, and electronic insulating properties, can form self-assembled films via non-valent bonding forces and have been considered a promising substitute for GF separator [27,203,209,210]. Although the application of cellulose-based separator has achieved some success in realizing economic and relatively long-life ZMBs in recent years,[57,185,211-213] it still needs to be modified to achieve the preferential (002) orientation growth of the deposited Zn metal. Therefore, Cao et al. developed a composite separator prepared from cellulose nanofibers and GO (CG) for long-life dendrites-free Zn anode [27]. From Fig. 13h, it is observable that the (002) crystal plane occupied a dominant position when employed as the CG separator. In this architecture, the negative surface charges and zincophilic-O functional groups on the surface of the CG separator promoted the strong interaction between the separator and the Zn species. Meanwhile, the high lattice matching between Zn (002) and GO (002) planes resulted in partial GO adsorption on the surface of the Zn anode and formed Zn deposits with hexagonal (002) basal planes. Subsequently, these Zn deposits with preferred (002) planes could induce the new deposition of Zn to grow along with the (002) orientation, resulting in a planar Zn deposition (Fig. 13i). Coincidentally, Luo et al. introduced a simple filtration method to obtain GO nanosheet-modified cellulose acetate (CA) separator with a low mass loading (4 μg cm−2) [214]. The CA/GO separator exhibited a similar regulatory mechanism that was reported by Cao et al.,[27] which was Zn-ion deposition along the parallel direction. Interestingly, by comparing the electrochemical performance and XRD patterns of Zn electrode using reduced GO and GO-modified separators, it could be confirmed that zincophilic-O functional groups were beneficial for the directional Zn deposition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface (002) texture design of Zn anode
ce:section.ce:para: As the hostless nature of Zn anodes, the surface atomic structure of Zn metal has an important effect on achieving reversible Zn deposition during continuous plating/stripping processes. Considering that the crystal orientation of the electrode surface constrains the growth direction of the newly deposited metal, however, the crystallographic heterogeneity of most commercial metal foils induces the morphological diversity of the deposited metal, showing unstable electrochemical performance [48]. Zn (002) plane has a compacted structure in atomic arrangement, exhibiting a smooth surface, while low surface energy and high binding energy suggest low nucleation overpotential and small voltage hysteresis. Therefore, building (002) texture on Zn anode directly to guide planar Zn deposition has emerged as a promising strategy to achieve long-life dendrite-free ZMBs.

Undoubtedly, using single-crystal (002) Zn as the anode in ZMBs is the most direct way to achieve planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition. The absence of grain boundaries and defects in the single-crystal structure and the perfect lattice matching of the (002) plane is the best choice for the homo-epitaxial growth of deposited Zn metal. More recently, Pu et al. reported planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition on single-crystal (002) Zn at ultra-high rates [215]. Fig. 14 a displays the SEM image of Zn deposited on the signal-crystal (002) Zn anode at a current density of 200 mA cm−2, which showed a smooth and dense planar Zn deposition. The extended field-of-view electron backscatter diffraction further characterized the crystalline structure of the signal-crystal (002) Zn anode after 200 mA cm−2 Zn plating, which confirmed that the Zn deposited on the single-crystal (002) Zn anode was smooth with only (002) orientation plane (Fig. 14b). In addition, the defect-free interface of the plated Zn and the single-crystal (002) Zn revealed the homo-epitaxial Zn deposition (Fig. 14c). This work strongly demonstrated the effect of electrode surface defects on the electrochemical performance during Zn deposition and the importance of lattice matching to achieve planar dendrite-free Zn deposition. Meanwhile, it also further confirmed that the crystal homogeneity of the metal electrode was transformed into the homogeneity of the deposited metal morphology. Although there are many advantages of using single-crystal (002) Zn as the anode of ZMBs, its high cost needs to be considered, otherwise, it loses its low-cost competitive advantage.

The mechanical roll forming process refers to the use of the principle of rolling extrusion to achieve the customization of the target material by relying on the plastic flow characteristics of the material. Zn is of hcp structure, having a large value of c/a axial ratio (c/a = 1.856), higher than the ideal value of 1.633. Early studies have shown that the c/a ratio of Zn approaches the ideal value with increasing the applied pressure, indicating that the metal tends to have a close-packed structure at high pressure, exhibiting a more isotropic structure [216,217]. Meanwhile, combined with the metal slip mechanism, the metal slip deformation was most likely to occur on the plane with the highest atomic density and interplanar spacing [218], that is, the slip of the Zn (002) plane along the <110> direction [48,219]. Therefore, the strategy of realizing (002)-textured Zn electrode by roll forming process came into being.

For example, Wu et al. introduced an economical and scalable method to fabricate metal nanosheets by self-folding and rolling a single metal foil to achieve metal nano/microsheets with lateral dimensions ranging from hundreds of nanometers to several micrometers and nanoscale thicknesses [220]. The obtained Zn metal microsheets by this preparation process showed higher RTC of the Zn (002) plane than the standard hexagonal Zn phase, which indicated that a surface-preferred (002) plane was prepared. Meanwhile, the carboxymethylcellulose-derived carbon-coated Zn microsheets (Zn MS@C) displayed an excellent HER inhibition performance. The XRD of the Zn MS@C electrode after long-term cycles still maintained a high RTC of the Zn (002) plane, suggesting that the newly deposited metal was along the (002) orientation. Zhou et al. prepared a Zn anode with a preferentially exposed (002) plane by using a rolling method with gradually reducing the temperature and increasing the rotational speed of the pre-cast Zn metal ingot [49]. Obviously, the Zn anode with more exposed (002) plane had a smoother equipotential surface and a denser architecture, which was beneficial to the flat Zn deposition, thereby delaying the dendrite growth. As a result, Zn electrodes exposing more (002) texture were in sharp contrast to Zn metal exposed (100) texture in retarding dendrite growth, inhibiting HER, and corrosion resistance (Fig. 14d). Furthermore, Zheng et al. reported a room-temperature accumulative roll bonding technology to realize a highly (002)-textured Zn electrode and proposed a crystal plane slip mechanism based on metal plastic deformation (Fig. 14e) [48]. It is evident that the brightness of the (002) plane in the 2D XRD pattern increased when the metal foil was rolled a number of times (Fig. 14f). More importantly, this method enabled the fabrication of ultrathin electrodes to meet the N:P ratio required for commercial batteries. Additionally, the plastic deformation was confirmed to have the same effect in achieving homogeneous Li/Na metal, confirming its scalability.

Direct thermal annealing of Zn metal foil under a protective atmosphere is another method for preparing (002) plane-dominated Zn electrodes. For example, Zhou et al. fabricated Zn electrodes with a more exposed (002) plane by an annealing process [49]. Similarly, Wang et al. exposed Zn metal foil to 5% Ar/H2 gas at 300 °C for 60 min and obtained a Zn electrode with a high texture coefficient of the Zn (002) plane (Fig. 14g) [221]. Meanwhile, under the protection of the pre-prepared zinc hydroxide sulfate film, the Zn electrode with the preferred (002) plane showed a lower hydrogen evolution rate (Fig. 14h), which is undoubtedly beneficial to the electrochemical performance of the Zn electrode.

Metal surface phosphating technology is one of the most widely used methods for metal surface treatment, which involves the formation of a phosphate film on the surface of the metal after phosphate treatment, thereby showing excellent corrosion resistance [222,223]. Recently, Wang et al. reported a simple phosphoric acid etching approach to preparing Zn electrode with a more exposed (002) plane [224]. Benefiting from the low surface energy of the Zn (002) surface, the acid etching preferentially occurred on the Zn (100) and Zn (101) planes (Fig. 14i), realizing the selective etching of the metal and exposing more (002) planes on the Zn substrate. Meanwhile, the etched product (zinc phosphate) was anchored on the Zn metal surface (PPZ@Zn) as a corrosion-resistant coating layer to protect the Zn metal, which made the Zn electrode maintain a smooth surface after long-term plating/stripping (Fig. 14j) and long-term stability electrochemical performance. Similarly, Liu et al. proposed a phosphoric acid etching of Zn metal to achieve a Zn electrode dominated by (002) texture and employed phosphoric acid as an electrolyte additive to further achieve long-term stable plating/stripping [225]. Benefiting from the exposure of the (002) plane of the Zn electrode and the high ionic conductivity of zinc phosphate, the homogeneous epitaxial growth of the Zn metal on the electrode surface was achieved. Subsequently, Su et al. further investigated the mechanism by which phosphoric acid selectively etched Zn foil to form the preferred Zn (002) plane [226]. As shown in Fig. 14k, the high atomic coordination and interatomic bonding of the Zn (002) plane endowed its stronger resistance to acid etching. Therefore, the phosphoric acid selectively etched the Zn (100) surface on the Zn foil and formed hydrogen and zinc phosphate. Then, the formed zinc phosphate could be dissolved in the phosphoric acid solution and removed during water wash. As a result, a stripy Zn array anode with a preferential (002) crystal plane was prepared (Fig. 14l), which delivered an ultra-stable Zn plating/stripping for 1400 h with the current density of 5.0 mA cm−2 and the areal capacity of 5.0 mAh cm−2.

It is not difficult to find that the Zn electrode with a more exposed (002) plane is superior to the commercial Zn metal anode of crystallographic heterogeneity in retarding dendrite growth, inhibiting side reactions. Compared with the direct thermal annealing and acid etching technologies, the possible influencing factors (rolling temperature, Zn foil thickness, time, etc.) have been discussed by predecessors in their research work on the crystallographic homogenization of Zn electrodes based on the rolling technology, so the rolling method seems more mature. It is easy to find that the thermal annealing of Zn metal is a simple method to achieve (002)-textured Zn electrode. However, the microcracks on the electrode surface also appear as the heat treatment continues,[227] which increases the local field intensity distribution on the electrode surface, induces the deposition of Zn metal at the cracks, and leads to dendrite growth [228]. Therefore, other auxiliary measures are necessary. Meanwhile, the phosphoric acid etching method also has some variables to consider, such as the concentration of phosphoric acid, electrode soaking time, ambient temperature, etc.

The renaissance of aqueous ZMBs marks that the development of materials and technologies today can solve the problems that existed in ZMBs before, which was considered to hinder their development and was difficult to create a breakthrough. Nevertheless, there are still some challenges to realizing ZMBs with excellent electrochemical performance to satisfy the needs of commercial applications. Among them, the problems of side reactions and dendrite growth of the Zn anode seriously affect the long-term stable electroplating/stripping of the Zn electrode, resulting in the poor electrochemical performance of the ZMBs. Benefiting from the unique atomic arrangement structure of the Zn (002) crystal plane, it exhibits the potential for corrosion resistance and dendrite suppression, laying the foundation for planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition. However, limited by the crystal heterogeneity of commercial Zn foil, it can easily lead to inhomogeneous deposition morphology, triggering dendrite growth and unexpected side reactions, especially in meeting the more severe test conditions required for commercial applications. In this review, the corresponding strategies to realize high-performance Zn anode with more exposed (002) basal plane have been systematically summarized, including artificial interface layer design, electrolyte optimization, separator innovation, and Zn substrate surface texture construction. Herein, we systematically summarize the general principles of the above strategies to achieve the preferred (002) plane-oriented Zn deposition, and further propose their respective advantages and disadvantages.
1)
Constructing an ideal artificial interface layer is an effective method for suppressing side reactions and delaying Zn dendrite on the electrode surface. Certainly, in addition to meeting the general requirements of an ideal interfacial layer, the following factors also need to be considered to realize the Zn metal deposition along the (002) plane. For example, the designed interfacial layer with a high lattice match to the Zn (002) can achieve the homo-epitaxial Zn deposition; the artificial interface layer can enhance the Zn ion 3D diffusion, balance the electric field, and reduce the Zn ion concentration gradient on the electrode surface, thereby eliminating the tip effect; the artificial interfacial layer can reduce the Zn-ion desolvation barrier and enhance cation migration kinetics, resulting in rapid Zn deposition. However, there are some shortcomings in constructing an artificial interface layer on the Zn anode. The interface layer formed by the ex situ coating method has poor adhesion to Zn substrate, which makes the coating layer prone to detaching and difficult to withstand the long-term Zn plating/stripping process. Meanwhile, a thick coating layer will decrease the volumetric energy density of ZMBs. In contrast, the in situ formed interfacial layers tend to exhibit uniform and dense architecture as well as good adhesion to the substrate. Obviously, this method often requires more complex preparation processes and more advanced instruments to characterize.
2)
As the most widely used mild aqueous electrolyte in ZMBs, the overall electrochemical performance of the ZnSO4 electrolyte is inferior to that of the Zn(OTf)2 electrolyte. The high cost of Zn(OTf)2, however, hampers its large-scale application. In addition, the Zn anode still suffers the issues of dendrite and side reactions when employing these two electrolyte systems, especially against high current density and areal capacity test conditions. Electrolyte additive is one of the most common and effective approaches to address these issues, which also plays a significant role in achieving directional Zn deposition. For the organic additives, it is necessary to have high adsorption energy toward the Zn anode surface and reduce the surface energy of the Zn (002) plane, resulting in eliminating the tip effect and promoting the Zn metal directional deposition along the (002) plane. Meanwhile, it can adjust the Zn-ion solvation structure, thereby reducing the desolvation energy barrier, improving the reversibility of electrodes, and inhibiting side reactions. As for the inorganic additives, it is required to own high binding energy with the Zn (002) plane to induce planar Zn deposition, and/or to form a lattice-matched interface with the Zn (002), prompting the deposited metal to expose more (002) plane. However, the additive will sometimes decrease the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte, which affects the reaction kinetics of the Zn ions. Meanwhile, the viscosity of the electrolyte increases with the additive concentration, and the additive may form a passivation layer on the metal surface, resulting in an obvious voltage hysteresis phenomenon.
3)
The separator is a vital component in the battery system, which also plays an essential role in affecting the Zn plating/stripping process and the electrochemical performance of ZMBs. However, common separators are difficult to suppress dendrite growth and cannot regulate Zn-ion uniform transport. Thus, separator modification is employed to further improve the electrochemical performance of ZMBs. Introducing heteroatom doped graphene or GO coating layer is considered an effective strategy to achieve directional Zn deposition, which involves the lattice matching between graphene (002) and Zn (002) crystal planes. In addition, the abundant zincphilic-O functional groups contribute to the redistribution of Zn ions on the Zn anode surface, resulting in the metal deposition in the horizontal direction. However, the strength enhancement of GF by these modification strategies is limited, which may not suppress dendrites enough. In addition, the thickness of the modified separator will increase, which may affect the volume energy density of the ZMBs in practical applications.
4)
The (002)-textured Zn electrode is considered the most direct way to achieve preferential Zn deposition along the (002) orientation. Compared with the commercial Zn anode, the (002)-textured Zn anode delivers low surface energy and high binding energy, thus feeding back low nucleation overpotential and small voltage hysteresis in Zn symmetric battery. Several strategies have been employed to construct (002)-textured Zn electrode, including rolling, etching, and thermal annealing metal anode. The rolling technology can achieve a crystallographic homogenization of Zn anode through the crystal plane slip, resulting in a more isotropic structure. Additionally, the roll forming process can prepare an ultrathin Zn electrode with a required N:P ratio for commercial batteries to improve the Zn utilization. Although the etching and thermal annealing of the Zn metal anode can also obtain a preferred (002) plane Zn anode, it is difficult to circumvent the surface defects of the Zn anode, which affects long-term stable Zn plating/stripping. Undoubtedly, the single-crystal (002) Zn anode, with the absence of defects and perfect (002) plane, is the best choice for the homo-epitaxial growth of deposited Zn metal. However, the high cost will hinder its large-scale application.

Therefore, under the premise of satisfying the uniform electric field distribution and Zn ion flow at the electrode/electrolyte interface and less/no side reactions, the common principle of realizing the (002) crystal plane orientation Zn deposition can be further summarized as follows. The newly metal is deposited parallel to the substrate direction by regulating the orientation of Zn ions before deposition, which is mostly achieved by lattice matching and homogeneous ion channels. On the other hand, Zn ions are preferentially deposited on the (002) plane of the Zn anode mainly by reducing the surface energy and increasing the adsorption energy of the Zn (002) plane, which lays the foundation for the subsequent planar Zn deposition. The related electrochemical performances are further summarized in Table 1 . Although the research on the preferred Zn (002) crystal plane has made significant progress in realizing long-term stable Zn anode, there are still many challenges. Herein, we proposed some personal perspectives for the potential future development of this field:
1)
The deposition mechanism of Zn at the electrode-electrolyte interface to form the preferred (002) plane still needs further study. Early studies on Zn electrodeposition were most executed in an open environment, and most current Zn metal deposition mechanisms relied on the results from Li/Na metal batteries. However, in the coin cell battery and pouch cell battery, attendant issues such as the closed battery system, electrode surface pressure, and the difference between the electrolyte and electrochemical reactions need to be considered. Moreover, most of the current research focuses on room temperature, and further research on deposition mechanisms in high- or low-temperature environments is valuable. Therefore, more in-depth studies based on in situ and ex situ characterization methods as well as advanced electrochemical methods are necessary to reveal the mechanism of the Zn metal directional deposition.
2)
Constructing (002)-textured Zn electrode is the most direct route to realize the planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition. Although the rolling method is beneficial for exposing more Zn (002) plane and preparing ultrathin Zn electrodes, it is necessary to consider the influence of the extra stress brought by the rolling process on the deposition of Zn metal. Fortunately, the effect of Zn electrode surface stress on the Zn metal deposition has been considered in recent years [228,229]. It is believed that as more researchers pay attention to the problem of electrode surface stress, the stress-free/less stress (002)-textured Zn electrode has huge research potential. Meanwhile, surface defects of Zn foil could induce uneven Zn deposition, seriously affecting the cycle life of the electrode, which also needs to be considered.
3)
Although various strategies have been explored to achieve planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition, there is still a lack of deep understanding of the general strategies and specific mechanism of action to achieve the Zn (002) plane. In addition, the thermal runaway of the ZMBs may occur during high-power operation, which affects the battery cycle life. Moreover, in the process of stabilizing the Zn anode, the internal electric field in the ZMBs may be altered, which impacts the Zn metal deposition behavior. However, these issues are easily neglected when studying ZMBs. Therefore, it is valuable to combine simulations and theoretical calculations to reveal the effect of the Zn anode with the preferred (002) plane on the morphology of the newly deposited metal. Meanwhile, the real-time monitoring through advanced in-situ visualization techniques will help to further understand the morphology and mechanism of Zn deposition on the Zn (002) plane.
4)
The current research on Zn metal anodes is mainly based on having excess Zn. Although the deep discharge test can reflect the utilization of the Zn anode, the practical capacity is higher than the N:P ratio required for commercial batteries. Therefore, it is crucial to employ ultrathin metal anodes to keep the metal electrode fully utilized during cycling. However, only a few studies have attempted to use ultrathin Zn metal electrodes to approach the requirement of commercial batteries,[48,230] which means that its practical application remains to be further explored.
5)
The influence of inconsistent criteria on evaluating the electrochemical performance of Zn electrode is important. For example, CE is an important reference index for evaluating electrode reversibility, which is the ratio of discharge capacity over charge capacity in LIBs. However, this calculation method is controversial in metal batteries. Xiao et al. discussed the problems existing in the CE test of Li metal batteries and proposed a new test method [231]. In addition, Ma et al. introduced a galvanostatic reservoir CE protocol for ZMBs, which involves the calibration of parameters such as current density, areal capacity, and upper cut-off voltage,[11] which is beneficial for the accuracy of CE testing. Therefore, the development of an industry-standard would be helpful for the commercial application of ZMBs, involving the electrolyte amount and the separator thickness. Moreover, the problem of soft short circuit in Zn symmetric battery needs attention [232], which usually exhibits long-term stable plating/stripping at ultra-high current density with ultra-high areal capacity, accompanied by a very small voltage hysteresis. Obviously, this is unconvincing.
6)
Considering the intrinsic safety properties of aqueous ZMBs, they have broad prospects in the flexible portable electronics market, especially as energy storage devices for wearable electronics. However, the advantages of the Zn (002) plane need to be further investigated in flexible electrodes. Meanwhile, exploring the interactive combination of different strategies may be helpful for realizing highly reversible Zn anodes closer to commercial applications.

Although Zn anode with more exposed (002) plane is beneficial for planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition, it still requires more detailed studies to meet its practical application of aqueous rechargeable ZMBs. This review could provide some new ideas for planar and dendrite-free Zn deposition under mild aqueous conditions, thereby promoting the development of advanced Zn anode. Eventually, the continued efforts of researchers will promote the commercial application of aqueous ZMBs.
